numpy_module.title: 1. Memory Slicing Visualization (4x4 Matrix)
numpy_module.view_desc: Basic Slicing always returns a view. Modifying it will change the original matrix memory directly!
numpy_module.fancy_desc: Fancy Indexing creates a copy. This involves memory allocation and is less efficient for large scale usage.
numpy_module.mask_desc: Boolean Mask also triggers memory copy. It is a powerful tool for extracting specific conditional data.
numpy_module.instruction: Select a slicing method to observe NumPy's memory behavior...
numpy_module.broadcast_advice: Broadcasting doesn't actually copy data in memory, but virtually maps addresses during iteration. Core rule: Compare from the last dimension forward, they must either be equal or one of them is <1>1</1>.

backprop_module.title: Chain Rule Tracker
backprop_module.step2_desc: Let <1>\delta_k = - \frac{\partial E}{\partial net_k}</1>. Under MSE loss:
backprop_module.advice: Remember, backpropagation is essentially a process of 'finding the culprit'. Each layer weight <1>w_{jk}</1> is asking: How much of the error <3>E</3> am I responsible for? <5>\delta_k</5> is the 'blame letter' passed back from the previous layer.

kernel_module.advice: A convolution kernel is essentially a local feature filter. When you use the <1>\text{Laplacian}</1> operator, it is approximating the second derivative and can quickly capture 'sudden changes' in pixel brightness.

terms.prior_desc: Probability of belief <1>B</1> before any evidence.
terms.likelihood_desc: Probability of evidence <1>E</1> given belief <3>B</3> is true.
terms.marginal_desc: Total probability of evidence <1>E</1> under all conditions.

fire_case.title: 2. Practice: Smoke & Fire Case (Notes P.15)
fire_case.advice: Classic case from notes: Fire is rare (Prior=1%), but smoke is common (Marginal=10%, maybe BBQ). Bayes tells us probability of fire given smoke is only <1>9\%</1>. <3>Evidence ≠ Conclusion, Prior matters!</3>

naive.advice: Key point from Notes P.23: Bayes is 'Naive' because it assumes features are <1>conditionally independent</1>. Efficient for spam filtering. Watch out for <3>Zero Frequency Problem</3>, increase <5>\alpha</5> to see changes!

knn.click_instruction: Click background to move test point
knn.advice: Observe the interaction: As you increase <1>K</1>, the decision boundary becomes smoother. Small <1>K</1> leads to complex boundaries prone to <3>Overfitting</3> (capturing noise), while large <5>K</5> simplifies the boundary causing **Underfitting**. Also, toggle 'Standardized' to see how scale affects distance—without it, features with larger ranges (like Weight) dominate the distance calculation, which is a classic trap in KNN!

exam_tip.title: Exam Tip
exam_tip.content_numpy: Exam Note: <1>Broadcasting</1> can only extend dimensions of Size 1. <3>A \cdot B</3> (np.dot) and <5>A \odot B</5> (Element-wise) have completely different physical meanings!
exam_tip.content_backprop: Exam Note: error flows backward — compute <1>\delta_k</1> at the output layer first. Memorize <3>\Delta w = \eta \delta O</3> and mind the sign of (T - O)!
exam_tip.content_kernel: Exam Note: with no padding and stride 1, output size is <1>N - K + 1</1>. Sobel-X responds to vertical edges — don't mix up the direction!
exam_tip.content_bayesBasics: Exam Note: <1>P(B|E) \propto P(B) \cdot P(E|B)</1>. The denominator P(E) only normalizes — compare hypotheses by the numerator!
exam_tip.content_naiveBayes: Exam Note: one zero frequency kills the whole product — apply <1>\alpha</1> smoothing. With many features, switch to <3>\log</3> sums to avoid underflow!
exam_tip.content_knn: Exam Note: choose an odd <1>K</1> to avoid tie votes, and always standardize features — otherwise large-range features dominate the distance!
exam_tip.content_alphabeta: Exam Note: pruning fires when <1>\beta \le \alpha</1>. Node visiting order matters — <3>a good move ordering prunes far more branches</3>!
exam_tip.content_kmeans: Exam Note: every iteration keeps <1>WCSS</1> non-increasing, so K-Means always converges — but only to a local optimum (initialization matters). Pick <3>K</3> at the elbow point!

explain.leaf: Evaluate leaf {{node}}: static value {{value}}, return to parent {{parent}}.
explain.traverse: Descend from {{node}} to child {{child}}, carrying [α={{alpha}}, β={{beta}}].
explain.prune: β({{beta}}) ≤ α({{alpha}}) at {{node}} → prune! Skip remaining branches [{{children}}].
explain.exit: Exit {{node}}: final score {{value}}, return to parent.

alphabeta.advice: Alpha-Beta returns the exact same value as plain Minimax, just faster. The prune condition <1></1> means the parent already has a better guaranteed option, so the rest of this branch can never affect the result. <3>Move ordering is everything</3>: evaluate the most promising child first to trigger maximum pruning.

kmeans.init_state: Initial state: points assigned to nearest of the initial centroids.
kmeans.moved: Centroids moved a total of {{d}} this step.
kmeans.elbow_hint: Inertia always drops as K grows; pick the 'elbow' where the marginal gain flattens.
kmeans.advice: K-Means alternates two steps (EM): assign each point to the nearest centroid, then move each centroid to its cluster mean. Inertia never increases. Choosing <1>K</1> is the hard part — use the <3>Elbow Method</3>. Always Z-score standardize first, or large-scale features dominate the Euclidean distance!
